turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Whether I had read it or not, I would not have believed it, that 3 million Jews had been killed. That is something I would not have believed, and that is why I left it out, at any rate. Anyhow, the German censorship would not have allowed the spreading of something which is not credible.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You didn't read the last part of the line, did you?

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: [Repeating.] ". . . were mentally and physically broken. That is the result of the new order." I am very much obliged to you. [Turning to the defendant.] "That is the result," you say, "of the 'new order' in Europe..." You say you didn't believe it. Is that what you say now, that you must have read it-must you not?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: But you just didn't believe it; is that right?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: No, I did not believe it.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Even if you didn't believe it, when you were reading this newspaper more or less regularly, when your cameraman had been to the ghettos in the East, did you think it right to go on, week after week, in your newspaper crying for the extermination, murder, of the Jews?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: That is not correct. It is not true that murder was demanded week after week. And I repeat again, the sharpening of our tone was the answer to the voice from America that called for our mass murder in Germany-eye for eye, tooth for tooth. If a Jew, Erich Kauffmann, demands mass murders in Germany, then perhaps I, as an author, can say that the Jews too should be exterminated. That is a literary matter. But the mass murders had taken place a long time before without our having known about them; and I state here that if I had known what had in fact happened in the East, then I would not have used these quotations at all.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: But, Defendant, you must have known then, must you not, after reading that article, after sending your cameraman, after the United Nations published their declaration, after Hitler's prophecies had been made again and again in his proclamations, after you said his prophecy had been fulfilled? You really say you didn't know?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: The cameraman is at your disposal. He is in Vienna, and I ask to have him brought here. And I state that this cameraman reported nothing, and could not have reported anything, about mass murders.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think we might adjourn now. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Mr. President, with the permission of the Tribunal, and in the interest of clarification of the facts, I should like to point out the following: The Prosecutor, Sir Griffith-Jones, has mentioned a document, Page 38-A from Der Stuermer of 6 May 1943. That seems to be an error, because we are dealing here with Der Stuermer of 6 March 1943. That date is of the greatest importance because if the photographer of Der Stuermer published a report of 6 March in Der Stuermer , then he must have been at the ghetto in Warsaw before 6 March 1943. Presumably... THE PRESIDENT Why do you say 6 March? The document I have before me has 6 May.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: There has been a mistake, I am afraid, in the German that Dr. Marx has. I have the original before me, which is 6 May 1943.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Excuse me. At the present moment I cannot recall when the destruction of the ghetto of Warsaw took place. That was Document 1061-PS.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: I cannot remember for the moment the number of the document, but the date was, I think from memory, from the 1st to the 23rd of April.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Then, of course, my remark is without foundation. Please excuse me.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Now we had just dealt with the Israelititsches Wochenblatt issue for 27 August, the copy that you quoted from. I just refer you to one more copy of that newspaper. Would you look at Page 37-B, which is an issue of 10 September 1943: "Statistics presented by the Convening Committee showed that 5 millions out of the 8.5 million Jews of Europe had died or been deported... About 3 million Jews had lost their lives through forced labor and deportation." Did you read that one?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: I do not know, and again I would not have believed it. To this day I do not believe that 5 million were killed. I consider it technically impossible that that could have happened. I do not believe it. I have not received proof of that up until now.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: It is quite clear that there were plenty of figures for you, quoted in this Israelitisches Wochenblatt over the period that we are discussing. Plenty of figures, it now turns out, doesn't it?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: We will go on. Now, I just want to put one or two further articles of your own to you. You remember what I am suggesting, that you are inciting the German people to murder. We know now that at least you had read one article in the Israelitisches Wochenblatt where murder is mentioned. I just want to see what you go on to publish in your own paper after that date. Would you look at Page 47-A. This is an article by yourself on 6 January 1944. This is after you had been living on your estate for some time. "After the National Socialist uprising in Germany, a development began in Europe, too, from which one can expect that it will free this continent for all time of the Jewish disintegrator and exploiter of nations; and, over and above this, that the German example will, after a victorious termination of the second World War, bring about the destruction of the Jewish world tormentor on the other continents as well." What example was the German nation setting to the other nations of the world? What example do you mean there?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: This article corroborates what I have been saying all along. I spoke of an international solution of the Jewish question. I was convinced that if Germany had won this war or had been victorious over Bolshevism, then the world would have agreed that an understanding should be reached with the other nations for an international solution of the Jewish question. If I wrote here about destruction, it is not to be understood as destruction by mass killing; as I have said, that is an expression; I have to point out that I do not believe that Erich Kauffmann really wanted to kill the German people by sterilization, but he wrote it, and we sometimes wrote in the same manner, echoing the sounds that we heard in the other camp.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: You have not yet told us what is this international solution that you are advocating by talking about extermination; if it is not murder, what is it? What is the solution?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: I have already said that I founded the Anti-Semitic Union, and through this Anti-Semitic Union we wanted to create movements among the nations which should, above and beyond governments, act in such a way that an international possibility would be created, such as has been represented today here in this Trial-thus I conceived it, to form an international congress center which would solve the Jewish question by the creation of a Jewish state and thereby destroy the power of the Jews within the nations.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: That is your answer-that you were advocating a Jewish state? Is that all that this comes to? Is it simply that you were advocating a Jewish national home? Is that what you have been talking about in all these extracts that we have read? Is that the solution which you are advocating?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Well, I do not know what you want with that question. Of course, that is the solution.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Very well. Let us just go on now. Turn to Page 48-A now, will you? This is 24 January 1944, "Whoever does what a Jew does is a scoundrel, a criminal, and he who repeats and wishes to copy him deserves the same fate- annihilation, death." Are you still advocating a national Jewish home?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, that has nothing to do with the big political plan. If you take every statement by a writer, every statement from a daily newspaper, as an example, and want to prove a political aim by it, then you miss the point. You have to distinguish between a newspaper article and a great political aim.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Very well, let us just turn now to the next page, 2 March 1944, "Eternal night must come over the born criminal race of Jews so that eternal day may bless awakening non-Jewish mankind." Were they going to have eternal night in their national Jewish state? Is that what you wanted?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: That is an anti-Semitic play of words. Again it has nothing to do with the great political aim.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: It may be an anti-Semitic play of words, but the only meaning it can have is murder. Is that not true?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Will you turn to the next page, 25 May 1944; and I remind you that these are all after you must have read of the murder in Israelitisches Wochenblatt . I quote the second paragraph: "How can we overcome this danger and restore humanity to health? Just as the individual human being is able to defend himself against contagious diseases only if he proclaims war against the cause of the disease, the germ, so the world can be restored to health only when the most terrible germ of all times, the Jew, has been removed. It is of no avail to battle against the outward symptoms of the world disease without rendering the morbific agents innocuous. The disease will break out again sooner or later. The cause and the carrier of the disease, the germ, will see to that. But if the nations are to be restored to health and are to remain healthy in the future, then the germ of the Jewish world plague must be destroyed, root and branch." Is that what you mean? Are you saying there when you say "must be destroyed root and branch"-did you mean to say "ought to be given a Jewish national state"?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, it is a far cry from such a statement in an article to the act, or to the will, to commit mass murder.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Turn over to the 10th of August. "When it loses this struggle, Judaism will be ruined, then the Jew will be extinguished. Then will Judaism be annihilated down to the last man." Are we to read from these words: Provide the Jews with a Jewish national state?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: That is a vision of the future. I would like to call it an expression of a prophetic vision. But it is not incitement to kill 5 million Jews. That is an opinion, a matter of belief, of conviction.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: It is the prophetic vision of what you wanted, is it not-of what you have been advocating now for the last 4 years-the beginning of the war? Isn't that what it is?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Mr. Prosecutor, I cannot tell you today what I may have been thinking years ago at a certain moment when writing an article. But still I admit that when I saw lying before me on the table declarations from the Jewish front, many declarations saying, "the German nation has to be destroyed; bomb the cities, do not spare women, children, or old men"-if one has declarations like these in front of one, it is possible that things will come from one's pen such as I have often written.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: You know, do you not, now, even if you do not believe the full figures, that millions of Jews have been murdered since the beginning of the war? Do you know that? You have heard the evidence, have you not?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: I believe it...

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: I only wanted to know whether you had heard that evidence. You can answer "yes" or "no," and I presume it will be "yes."

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, I have to say, evidence for me is only the testament of the Fuehrer. There he states that the mass executions took place upon his orders. That I believe. Now I believe it.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Do you think that it would have been possible to carry out the extermination of 6 million Jews in 1921? Do you think the German people would have stood for it? Do you think it would have been possible under any regime in 1921 to have carried out the murder of 6 million men, women, and children of the Jewish race?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Whether that would have been possible with the knowledge of the people-no, it would not have been possible. The prosecutor himself has said here that since 1937 the Party had full control over the people. Now even if the people had known this, according to the opinion of the Prosecution, they could not have done anything against that dictatorship because of that control. But the people did not know it. That is my belief, my conviction, and my knowledge.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Was it possible to exterminate people in that way only after some 20 years of incitement and propaganda by you and other Nazis? Is that what made that possible?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: I deny that the population was incited. It was enlightened, and sometimes a harsh word may have been directed against the other side as an answer. It was enlightenment, not incitement. And if we want to keep our place before history I have to state again and again that the German people did not want any killings, whether individually or en masse .

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: I am not going to let you go into another history about the German people. I am going to remind you of what you have said...

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: Adolf Hitler ..

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: I am going to remind you of what you said yesterday. I read from the transcript: You speak of a Jewish question at the time-that is 1923-"I would like to say that the public distinguished Jews only by their religion; to speak about a Jewish problem then would have been nonsense." Was that because there was no Jewish problem then, and that the Jewish problem had only been created by you and the Nazi regime?

turns.speaker: STREICHER
turns.text: It was my aim, and I reached that goal in part: If the laws which in the future should make impossible sexual intercourse between different races, that is to say if that should become law-then it would make the public realize that to be a Jew is not a point of religion but of people and race. I helped to create that basis. But mass killings were not the result of the enlightenment, or as the Prosecution say, incitement. Mass killings were the last acts of will of a great man of history who was probably desperate because he saw that he would not win.

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: I have no further questions. Perhaps I might be allowed to just sort out the exhibits and then mention to the Tribunal their numbers. If the Tribunal would agree, those that I have put in evidence, which are the other parts of the bundle other than I have actually quoted from-perhaps I could put them all in as one number and hand the exhibits in to the clerk, if that would be the convenient course.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think so, yes. If they are in one bundle and you are going to give one number to a number of documents, it had better be in one bundle, had it not?

turns.speaker: LT. COL. GRIFFITH-JONES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Marx, do you want to re-examine?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: I do not consider it necessary any more.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then the defendant can return to the dock. Dr. Marx, will you continue the defendant's case?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: I call now, with the permission of the Court, the witness Fritz Herrwerth. [The witness Herrwerth took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name?

turns.speaker: FRITZ HERRWERTH (Witness)
turns.text: Fritz Herrwerth.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: "I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing." [The witness repeated the oath in German.] You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How long have you known the Defendant Streicher?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Since the Party Rally in 1934.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: When did you enter his service and in what capacity?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I was employed on 15 October 1934, in Nuremberg, not in the personal service of Herr Streicher himself, but in the municipal motor pool. However, I worked for the then Gauleiter Streicher.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: When did you leave that service?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: In August 1943.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: For what reason?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: It was a personal dispute, and mainly due to my fault.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you have any other tasks to carry out for Herr Streicher?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And which?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Well, whatever came up. I also did agricultural work at the end.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Thus you were very often with Streicher?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And therefore you knew about the most important incidents during that period?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes. I do not know, however, what you call important incidents. There were things that I do not know about, that is, at least I assume that.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: I will ask you later in detail.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, if you please.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: The Defendant Streicher is accused of having caused acts of violence against the Jews and of having participated in these acts. Do you know of any such case?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Not a single one.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Will you please wait until the end of my question, and then I shall say "end of question." On 9 November 1938, did you drive Streicher back to Nuremberg from Munich, and when? End of question.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: It was on 9 November, yes. I do not know the time exactly. At that time Streicher left Munich a bit earlier, and it may have been about-I do not know for sure-9 o'clock perhaps.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did Streicher know already during that ride back that something was to be done that night against the Jewish population?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: No, he knew nothing about that.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Then, during the night of 9 November, did you witness a conversation between Streicher and the SA Leader, Von Obernitz?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Where did that conversation take place?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: In order to answer that question, I have to explain a little further. When Herr Streicher went to bed, I was usually with him or the house superintendent. On that evening Herr Stretcher went to bed earlier than usual. I do not know the reason. And that concluded my work for the day. I went from Herr Streicher to the Casino of the Gauleitung. That was in the cellar of the Gauleitung building on Schlageterstrasse. I played cards there. And then the former SA Obergruppenfuehrer, Von Obernitz, came and called me, as was customary, by the name of Fritz and told me he had to speak to Herr Streicher very urgently; and I answered him that Herr Streicher had already gone to bed. Then he said. "Then I must rouse him," and he told me he would assume the responsibility; it was an important affair. Herr Von Obernitz went to Herr Streicher's apartment in my car. Herr Streicher's bedroom is above my apartment. I had the keys and of course I could get in at any time. On the way to the apartment at night I noticed that many SA men were in the streets. I asked Herr Von Obernitz the reason for that. He told me that that night something was going to happen; the Jewish homes were to be destroyed. He did not say anything further to me. I accompanied Herr Von Obernitz all the way to the bed of Herr Streicher. Herr Von Obernitz then reported to Streicher about what was happening that night. I cannot recall the details very well any more, but I believe that he said that that night the Jewish homes were to be destroyed. Herr Streicher was, if I may say so, surprised. He had not known anything about it. He said literally to Herr Von Obernitz, and I remember that very clearly, "That is wrong. One does not solve the Jewish question that way. Do what you have been ordered. I shall have no part in it. If anything should occur so that you need me, then you may come for me." I can also mention that thereupon Herr Von Obernitz said that Hitler had declared that the SA should be allowed to have a fling as retribution for what had occurred in Paris in connection with Herr Vom Rath. Streicher stayed in bed and did not go out during that night.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did Herr Von Obernitz mention anything about the fact that the synagogues were to be set on fire?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I believe so, yes. But, as far as I remember, Herr Streicher refused to do that, too, because the synagogue, as far as I know, was burned down by the regular fire department, and upon orders from Herr Von Obernitz.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How do you know that?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I was there.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you watch it?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes. I was at the synagogue during the night.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And how could one assume that the regular fire department started the fire?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: How that could be assumed I do not know, but I saw it. The regular fire department started the fire.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Were you there in time to see how the fire was started or did you arrive when the building was already on fire?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: The building was not yet on fire, but the fire department was there already.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX.
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I can say nothing else.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did Herr Streicher at that time mention anything about the fact that he was afraid of a new wave of excitement on the part of the world press if the synagogue was burned? Did he say that that is why he refused to do it?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I believe so, yes, but I could not say definitely; but, if I remember correctly, they spoke about that.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did Obernitz say from whom he had received the order?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: He only repeated what Hitler had said-the SA should be allowed to have a fling.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Is it correct that you, Witness, told your wife during the same night about that conversation between Obernitz and Streicher?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I believe I did not speak about the conversation; but when I walked down from the second floor to the ground floor through my apartment, I told my wife that I would probably be a little late because that night that action was going to be started; I told her briefly what was happening but nothing about the conversation.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Then, later you were at the Pleikershof when Streicher had been forced to retire there or had retired? HERRWERTH Yes. .

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you remember an incident where the future Frau Streicher spoke about the incidents at Magdeburg which had occurred there the same night?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: No, I know nothing of that.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you not tell the then Frau Merkel that she should not talk about these incidents because Streicher always got very excited about them?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I can recall that Herr Streicher once said that he had been right in his opinion, for, not long after that night he received information-I do not know through whom-that, for instance, the glass for the window panes had to be bought from Holland again. Herr Streicher said then that that was the first confirmation of the correctness of the opinion he had expressed at that time.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Marx, just one moment. Sir David, would it be convenient to you and the counsel for the Defendant Von Schirach if we discussed the question about the documents at 0930 tomorrow morning?

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: My Lord, I will find out. Yes, counsel for Von Schirach says that he thinks it is all right.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well, 0930 tomorrow morning.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What observations did you make during your stay at Pleikershof about the attitude of Streicher with regard to the Jewish question? What was that about the Israelitisches Wochenblatt ?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Well, what do you want to know about the Israelitisches Wochenblatt ? Herr Streicher received it.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did he receive it regularly?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, I believe I can say that quite certainly. I always saw large bundles of newspapers of the Israelitisches Wochenblatt . They came continuously.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Herr Streicher said that during the first years of the war he had great difficulty in getting that paper and the Police did not release it easily.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, that can very well be. For I do not know, after all, of what year they were. I just saw them and it is difficult for me to tell now of what date these papers were.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Yes, you said there were always large bundles of them.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, on and off, but there were other newspapers too. Swiss newspapers were there, the Israelitisches Wochenblatt , and so on. There were always so many newspapers lying about and among them I saw here and there the Israelitisches Wochenblatt . I mean to say that it would not be possible for me to say how many there were.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: All right. Did Streicher speak at times about his knowledge of happenings in the East or of happenings in concentration camps in the East?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Well. Herr Streicher did not know anything at all about it. Thus he could not say anything about it. At least that is my conviction.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you, then, ever speak to him about it?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Not that I know of; I did not know anything about it myself.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you ever receive knowledge of a letter in which Streicher was reproached by Reichsfuehrer SS Himmler because he treated the French prisoners too well? Did you understand me?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, I understood, but I have to think about it. I know quite well that Herr Streicher once mentioned something about the treatment of prisoners. I know that the Frenchmen were treated very well, but whether the cause for that was a letter from Himmler I do not know.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: No, no. The cause for the good treatment, you mean?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: No, the cause for Herr Streicher's speaking about it. Herr Streicher spoke about reproaches against the good treatment of the Frenchmen; but I do not know whether the fact that he spoke about it was due to a letter from Himmler. But I do not believe that there was a single Frenchman who could complain in any way about the treatment.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: You were no longer present when the Frenchmen left?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know about an incident when the publisher Fink came into the garden of Streicher's home and admitted having lied to the police in an affair concerning shares?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: The question must be put in detail, Mr. Attorney, for I do not know all about it, only part of it. I know that the then Director Fink stood in tears before Streicher, that he wailed, that he accused himself, saying that he was a rascal and a traitor. But why, I do not know. For Herr Streicher then walked farther into the garden with him, and I only saw that Herr Fink wept, and again heard how he accused himself.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know that Streicher at certain intervals brought people from the SPD and the KPD (Social Democratic Party and Communist Party) from the Dachau Concentration Camp?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How many do you suppose there were?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I do not know. It was every year around Christmas time. I estimate that there were about 100 to 150 men every year. They came from Dachau. Herr Streicher had dinner prepared for them in a separate room, in the Hotel Deutscher Hof, and I believe that used to be the family reunion-that is to say, the prisoners rejoined the members of their family. Streicher also saw to it that released prisoners found work, and he intervened personally for them.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did he also get work for one or another of these released persons?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What do you know about that?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I remember that three men, I believe, came into the Mars motorcycle factory. Herr Streicher at that time told the plenipotentiary of the German Labor Front to find positions for these people, as far as I remember.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What was the attitude of Streicher when he found out that members of the Party had acquired cars and villas of Jewish property at very low prices?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I can still remember when Herr Streicher returned from Berlin. I do not know how much Herr Streicher knew at that time about these purchases; but at any rate, when Herr Streicher returned from Berlin where Herr Goering had expressed his views about these low-priced purchases of buildings, Herr Streicher, just arrived at the Nuremberg railroad station, said- and I heard it myself-that these purchases had to be nullified at once. Besides. I know only about one case where a Party member had to do with the purchase of a house. I do not know whether there were more of them.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know whether Streicher was under surveillance by the Gestapo while on his farm and that there was a prohibition against visiting him there?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: In answering the first question, I cannot say for sure that Criminal Police agents were there. I cannot affirm categorically that Herr Streicher was once under observation, but it could be safely assumed. I know of a woman who even stated that she had been photographed in the forest when she came from the railroad station to the farm. And what was the second question?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Whether people were prohibited from visiting him.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes. I met various members of the Party within the city and whomever I asked said to me, "Impossible to get out there, impossible to get out there." And if I asked who had issued the prohibition, then no one would talk about it; but as one heard it here and there, this prohibition was said to have been issued by the Deputy of the Fuehrer, Herr Hess.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know anything about the fact that Streicher, when he found out that acts of violence against Jews or other political adversaries were intended, stopped them immediately?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes. At least, on the basis of his statements. He always said that that was wrong.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know of any case where he took measures against somebody who had been a party to such acts of violence? If you do not know it, say you do not know.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Very well, at this moment I cannot recall any case.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know anything about that affair concerning the Mars Works shares? What do you know about it?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes. I know about that case through statements made by Streicher at that time. I was not a witness to these events myself, but Herr Streicher once related to me what had happened. Shall I describe it briefly?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Yes, but very condensed, please.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Streicher was in a Turkish bath at the time when the Director Fink and his adjutant, Koenig, came and offered to sell the shares to Herr Streicher. Herr Streicher said, "What kind of shares are they?" The answer was, "They are shares of the Mars Works." He said, "How many?" The answer was "100,000 marks' worth." Then Streicher said, 'what do the shares cost?" He was told "5,000 marks." Herr Streicher asked, "Why are these shares so cheap?" Finally Herr Fink said, I believe, "Because they are Jewish shares." Whoever knows Herr Streicher as I do, knows that Herr Streicher has never taken anything from a Jew. He protested very emphatically against the fact that such an offer had been made to him at all. That seemed to settle the matter for the time being, and then suddenly the then Gauleiter Herr Streicher had the thought that with that money he could possibly construct the third Gau building. He mentioned that to the gentlemen as they left, and they decided to buy the shares. Herr Streicher forbade them to use Party money. Then both did not know what to do. Herr Streicher said he would advance these 5,000 marks. That settled the case, but I had another experience later. It was about one and a half years after that trial that Streicher had had in Munich, when he was dismissed. At that time the wife of NSKK Obergruppenfuehrer Zuehlen came to me and asked whether I already knew that the criminal police was again in Nuremberg concerning the Streicher case. I said "no" to Frau Zuehlen and added, "If they want to find out something why do they not come out to the farm to Herr Streicher himself? He will give them all the necessary information." After about 2 to 3 weeks, I met the Director of Der Stuermer , Fischer, successor to Herr Fink. He told me-but I would like to mention first that the shares, together with the 5,000 marks, were confiscated from Herr Streicher. The then Director Fischer told me that on that same day he had received a phone call from the trustee association, and that the trustee association had reported to Director Fischer that they had transferred to the account of Der Stuermer the 5,000 marks which Streicher at that time had advanced for the purchase of the shares.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Marx, do you not think he is going into rather too much detail about this?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes, I will make it shorter. The man from the trustee association said that the 5,000 marks were released because the innocence of Streicher had been proved in this matter.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: You witnessed the Supreme Party Court session at that time?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What did Herr Fink say at that time? Did he not accuse himself again of having made false statements?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: I was not present when Herr Fink was questioned.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Very well. Then I would like to ask you, were you present when the incident in Munich occurred at the Kuenstlerhaus Inn-with the man who accosted Streicher?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Can you give us a description of how that incident occurred?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Well, Herr Streicher left the inn after dinner. I cannot remember the exact words any more, but I am going to try to describe it as well as possible. Herr Streicher left the inn, and as he went out that man approached Herr Streicher in a-may I say-improper manner. Streicher continued on his way and was silent at first. He asked the people around him, myself also, whether we knew that man. Nobody knew him. Then Herr Streicher sent his son, Lothar, back into the room again to speak to the man and to ask him what the reason was for such behavior. Lothar Streicher came out and said that the man had behaved in just the same manner again.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Will you please be more brief? You should only tell us how that incident occurred and what caused you and also Herr Streicher to use violence against the man.

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: You mean his behavior?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Yes. What happened then?

turns.speaker: HERRWERTH
turns.text: Herr Streicher asked the landlord for a room, and in that room Streicher spoke to the man personally. There again the man made offensive remarks, and then it came to blows, first with Lothar Streicher. Now, as it happened, he was a strong man, and of course all of us helped to get him down.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: All right. I am through with the questioning of this witness, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any of the Defense Counsel want to ask any questions? Do any of the Prosecution Counsel wish to cross-examine? Then the witness can retire. [The witness left the stand.]

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Then I should like to call the witness Wurzbacher, if he is available. Is he not? I do not know which one of the witnesses is still in the witness room. Is there anyone? Wurzbacher? Hiemer?

turns.speaker: MARSHAL (Colonel Charles W. Mays)
turns.text: Frau Streicher is available.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Is not the witness Wurzbacher here?

turns.speaker: MARSHAL
turns.text: I will see, Sir. He was not here a while ago. He was not called for.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What other witnesses have you got, Dr. Marx?

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: The wife of the defendant could be called as a witness now.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well, let her be called then.

turns.speaker: MARSHAL
turns.text: The witness Strobel is available now.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr: Marx wants to call Frau Streicher.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Excuse me, Mr. President. If it is rather difficult to call Frau Streicher, then the witness... [The witness Frau Streicher took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you give me your full name?

turns.speaker: FRAU ADELE STREICHER (Witness)
turns.text: Adele Streicher, born Tappe.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: "I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing." [The witness repeated the oath in German.] You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Your maiden name is Tappe and you were born in Magdeburg?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Were you a member of the NSDAP or of the Frauenschaft?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: When did you become Herr Streicher's secretary and for how long were you in that job?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: On 7 June 1940, I became Julius Streicher's secretary and I remained in that job until the end of the war.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And during that period, you were continuously on his farm?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, I was always with him.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Were you also in charge of all the correspondence for Herr Streicher?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What did that correspondence mainly consist of?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Mainly letters to his sons and to relatives.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What were Streicher's activities during that period of 5 years?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Julius Streicher did mainly physical work; that is, agriculture and gardening, and from time to time he wrote articles for Der Stuermer .

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: During these 5 years did he leave the farm at all or was he ever absent from the farm for any length of time?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: During the first few years of his stay there Julius Streicher did not leave the farm at all; later, once in a while, he would pay a visit in the neighborhood. His longest absence did not comprise an entire day and never a single night.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did you know that it was prohibited for prominent Party members to visit Herr Streicher?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, there was such a prohibition.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How did you know that?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: From conversations. Then, too, I myself remember, when Dr. Goebbels visited the farm, that Julius Streicher said to him, "Doctor, you dare to come here? Do you not know that it is prohibited by the Party chiefs to visit me?"

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: When did the visits of Dr. Ley and Dr. Goebbels occur?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Dr. Ley came to the farm on 7 May 1944. The visit of Dr. Goebbels occurred on 4 June 1944.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Would you please describe the character of these visits and what was the subject of the conversations?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Both visits were of a rather unofficial character. Dr. Ley wanted mainly to know how Julius Streicher was doing, personally. No political questions were raised. Ley said only, "Stretcher, the Fuehrer is waiting for you."

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And what did Streicher say to that?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Julius Streicher answered that he had become accustomed to his solitude, that he was happy as a farmer, and that Ley should tell the Fuehrer that he, Streicher, wanted nothing more. At the visit of Dr. Goebbels the subject of the conversation dealt mainly with Julius Streicher's dismissal from his office as Gauleiter, and Dr. Goebbels was of the opinion that Julius Streicher should return into the circle of old Party members; but he gave him the same answer, "Tell the Fuehrer I wish for nothing."

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Were you always present during these conversations?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Was not the Jewish question a subject of these conversations?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No, they never spoke about the Jewish question.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did they not speak about the happenings in the Eastern territories or in the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No, that never came up any more.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did not Streicher speak to you about the articles he intended to write for Der Stuermer , and did he not also speak about what he considered to be the solution of the Jewish problem?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: From all conversations with Julius Streicher I could see with certainty that he never thought of the solution of the Jewish question in terms of violence, but hoped for the emigration of Jews from Europe and their settlement in territories outside Europe.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Was Herr Streicher in correspondence with leading personalities of the Party or of the State?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No, neither personally nor by correspondence was there any such connection.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: I will now mention several names, of whom I want you to tell me whether they had any connection with him: Himmler, Heydrich, Bormann, or other leading men of the Police or the SS or the Gestapo.

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No, I know nothing of any of these men. With the exception of one letter from Herr Himmler there was never any mail.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: What was the reason for that letter?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: In that letter Herr Himmler complained about the fact that the French prisoners of war who were employed on our Pleikershof farm were treated too well.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How was the treatment of the prisoners of war and the foreign civilian workers on the farm?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: On the Pleikershof eight French prisoners of war, one Polish girl, and one Slovene girl were employed. They were all treated very well and very humanely. Each service for which Julius Streicher asked, each piece of work for which he asked personally, was especially rewarded with tobacco, pastry, fruit, or even money. Such cordial relations developed with some of the Frenchmen during the years that they were there that they assured us, with tears in their eyes at their departure, that they would visit Julius Streicher after the war with their families.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did Streicher not finally receive credible information about these mass executions in the East?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: I believe he found out about it through Swiss newspapers in 1944. We were never informed about it officially.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: But it is asserted that he already had knowledge before that.

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: You do not know anything about it?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: I only know about the Swiss newspapers.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Very well. You once brought up the subject, in a conversation, that in Magdeburg, from the 9 to 10 November 1938, you witnessed the demonstration against the Jews and that you were revolted by it. Is that true?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, I spoke about it and said that I was shocked at this action. Julius Streicher got very excited during that conversation and said, "Such nonsense occurred in Nuremberg also. That is not anti-Semitism; that is just great stupidity."

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Is it correct that Herr Streicher was hardly interested in the financial affairs of the publishing firm and left these things to the manager?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Julius Streicher never bothered about financial affairs at all, neither in the house nor in the firm. Again and again the gentlemen of the firm were disappointed when they wanted to report about annual balances or the like and Julius Streicher would tell them, "Do not worry me with your business matters. There are other things besides that are more important than money."

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How did he take care of the household expenses, then?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: I received 1,000 marks every month from the firm. That provided for the household, presents, and so on.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Do you know that he is supposed to have acquired shares through illegal pressure against a Jewish banker?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: That is completely out of the question. I consider it quite impossible that Julius Streicher acquired shares that way. I believe that he does not even know what a share looks like.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Did he not tell you anything about it?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: I only heard that he never received shares.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How did it come about that you and the defendant were married as late as April 1945? Did you understand the question?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes. Julius Streicher wanted to take part in the fighting in Nuremberg. I wanted to accompany him, so he married me before we left. We wanted to die together.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Then you left the Pleikershof with him, and where did you go from there?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: First we wanted to go to Nuremberg, and that was refused for fear of difficulties with the authorities. So we drove in the direction of Munich. In Munich we were told to continue in the direction of Passau. From Passau they sent us to Berchtesgaden; from Berchtesgaden they sent us to Kitzbuehel.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: How did it happen that the original intention to die together was not followed up? What caused him to change his mind?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: The cause for that was a conversation with three young soldiers.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: And what was that? I will be through right away, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I do not think you should go into that, Dr. Marx.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Well, then. I will forego the question. Only one more question: Is it correct that Streicher gave the managers of his publishing firm a written power of attorney which meant that they could dispose of the money as they saw fit?

turns.speaker: FRAU STREICHER
turns.text: Yes, Julius Streicher gave the power of attorney to whoever happened to be the manager of the firm, and thereby gave him his full confidence without any restrictions.

turns.speaker: DR. MARX
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no more questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do any of the defendants' counsel want to ask any questions? Does the Prosecution wish to ask any questions?

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then the witness can retire, and the Court will adjourn until 0930 tomorrow morning. [The Tribunal adjourned until 30 April 1946 at 0930 hours.]
